If the experiments are a pre-condition, they should be a separate child ticket or explicitly called out as phase-1 work within the epic. (2) The INDETERMINATE outcome is defined in the Design summary and the AC's routing bullet, but no Success Criterion explicitly validates it (the 6 success criteria do not mention INDETERMINATE at all), creating a gap between design intent and success measurement. (3) 'Bugs are exempt' is stated in the Design summary and AC, but no AC describes the detection/routing mechanism for bug tickets at the gate — an executor cannot implement the exemption without knowing how to identify bug tickets.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Explicitly sequence the experiments as a prerequisite or Phase 1 child ticket, and note that chunking/budget defaults are TBD until experiment results are available. (2) Add a Success Criterion: 'Budget exhaustion during review produces an INDETERMINATE outcome that is non-blocking by default and configurable.' (3) Add an AC sub-bullet: 'Bug tickets are detected by [field/tag/type=bug] and routed to PASS-through at the gate without review.'

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 bullet points) + 'Follow-on work' section listing F1–F12
findings.finding: This is a large, multi-layer epic with at least 12 distinct acceptance criteria spanning: a deterministic Layer-1 floor with multiple tool integrations (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard), a Layer-2 advisory engine with ~12 named criteria, a criteria registry + orchestrator, an attestation/signing subsystem, claim-gate integration, configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget, a sidecar event system, per-criterion config, docs, and experiment-grounded defaults. No decomposition into child tickets is present in the plan itself — the follow-on list (F1–F12) names exclusively post-epic work, not the breakdown of this epic's own work. As scoped, this epic is far too large for a single coherent agent session and should itself be decomposed into vertical slices (e.g., Layer-1 structural floor; Layer-2 advisory engine; attestation + claim integration; observability sidecar; config surface; docs/defaults).
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit child-ticket decomposition section to this epic. One possible vertical-slice breakdown: (1) Layer-1 deterministic floor + fail-open tooling harness; (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria engine + criteria registry/orchestrator; (3) Attestation signing + material-event-commit binding + claim integration; (4) Sidecar REVIEW_RESULT event + reducer skip; (5) Configurable chunking/routing/escalation/budget knobs + experiment-grounded defaults; (6) Docs. Each child should be independently claimable and deliverable.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)' and 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep + syft + scc + lizard'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly invokes existing rebar.llm extension points (catalog/contracts/prompt/runner) and existing repo tooling (solidlsp), which is the right anti-NIH instinct. However, two areas warrant verification against the live codebase: (1) Whether the 'generic orchestrator' for criteria is truly reusing existing rebar.llm runner infrastructure or is introducing a new parallel orchestration layer at a single call-site (Rule-of-Three risk). (2) Whether the 'criteria registry' (declarative descriptors) duplicates an existing configuration/registry pattern already in rebar. Without codebase access these cannot be confirmed as genuine reuse vs. gratuitous new abstraction.
findings.suggested_edit: Before implementing, verify: (a) that rebar.llm's existing runner/orchestrator can be extended rather than wrapped; (b) that the criteria-descriptor schema is not duplicating an existing config format. Add a note in the plan explicitly naming the specific existing extension points being reused.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all 12 bullets; 'Success Criteria' section
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan whatsoever. There is no mention of: unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks (including fail-open behavior under tool crash/timeout/unsupported-lang), behavioral tests for Layer-2 advisory criteria output (coaching-spec conformance), integration tests for the claim-gate attestation check, tests for attestation invalidation on material edits, tests for the sidecar event emission and reducer-skip behavior, or RED-before-GREEN ordering for any of these. Given the plan's own design principle that ~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects, the absence of a testing strategy for the plan-review gate itself is a significant gap.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing Plan' section (or per-AC testing notes) covering at minimum: (1) Unit tests for each Layer-1 check with a fail-open harness (inject tool crash, timeout, unsupported language → assert PASS/skipped); (2) Snapshot/golden tests for Layer-2 coaching output asserting grounding, severity, and checklist form; (3) Integration test: claim on a ticket with absent/stale attestation → blocked; claim with valid attestation → allowed; claim with --force + justification → allowed + logged; (4) Sidecar event schema test: assert reducer skips REVIEW_RESULT type; (5) Material-edit invalidation test: edit description → attestation marked stale.

findings.location: Multiple locations: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; 'extends the close-gate signing'; 'reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan makes several concrete assertions about existing codebase state that an executor would need to verify before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — assumed present and usable; (2) 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — assumes these extension points exist and are extensible in the required ways; (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes a close-gate signing mechanism exists with an extensible interface; (4) 'reducer skips the type' — assumes the reducer has a type-filtering/ignore mechanism already. Any of these being absent or incompatible would require rework. The plan references a session log ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') as the source of truth for these details, but that log is not accessible in the plan text.
findings.suggested_edit: For each assumption, add a brief verification note (e.g., 'verified: solidlsp is importable at rebar/tools/solidlsp.py'). If the session log is the source of verification, extract the key confirmed facts into the plan so an executor doesn't need to chase the log.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1: 'file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep)'; Design summary: 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'; 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan does not name specific files or modules to be created/modified. For an epic this is partially acceptable, but several specific integration points are asserted without naming the target: (1) where the claim command lives and which file/function will be modified to add the attestation check; (2) where the close-gate signing lives that this epic 'extends'; (3) which rebar.llm modules expose the catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points. Without codebase access, it cannot be confirmed whether these targets are plausible or hallucinated. The blast radius of touching the 'claim' command (used by all agents for all tickets) is high and is acknowledged in the design ('FAST check'), which is appropriate — no penalty for that.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules to be created or modified' subsection listing at minimum: the claim command entry point, the close-gate signing module being extended, the rebar.llm runner module, and the event reducer. This helps an executor start without hunting.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section tensions: (1) Design summary 'LAYER 1 BLOCKS' vs. AC 'fail-open'; (2) 'defaults grounded in experiment results' (AC bullet 11) vs. 'Execution: Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data'; (3) Follow-on F10 'calibrated abstention + labeled gold set' vs. current design's advisory-only posture
findings.finding: Three potential internal tensions worth flagging: (1) The design states Layer-1 'BLOCKS' while also stating 'fail-open on unsupported language/tool'. These are not contradictory but the plan conflates 'blocks when a check fires' with 'passes when a check can't run' — this distinction is clear in the design summary but the AC wording ('blocks only on sound, unambiguous checks and fails open') could be read as meaning Layer-1 never actually blocks, which would contradict the gate's purpose. A clarifying sentence in the AC would remove ambiguity. (2) AC bullet 11 requires 'defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' but the experiment work is listed as a dependency of this epic (implied by 'see linked experiment work') without being explicitly scoped as a prerequisite or child. If the experiments haven't run, the defaults can't be set, creating a hidden ordering dependency. (3) These are relatively minor tensions rather than true contradictions; none appear to make the plan internally incoherent at a critical level.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) In AC Layer-1 bullet, add: 'fail-open means the check is skipped (= PASS); blocking only fires when the check runs and a violation is detected.' (2) Explicitly list the experiment work as a prerequisite ticket or in-scope child task so the ordering dependency is visible to an executor.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution' bullet; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Several concrete design decisions are deferred to an external artefact or to empirical results that are not yet in scope. Specifically: (1) 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' with the experiments listed as a separate AC item referencing 'linked experiment work' — the actual default values are not specified in the plan, so an executor must wait for or interpret external results. (2) 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data; per-plan budget cap' — no numeric or formulaic definition of 'generous' or the cap formula is given. (3) 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' — the thresholds or algorithm for escalation are unspecified. (4) The 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' in Layer-2 AC are not described; what triggers them, or which overlays exist, is entirely open. These are not fatal blockers on their own (some are intentional configuration surface), but collectively they mean an executor will need to make non-trivial design choices without guidance, risking divergence from the intended behaviour.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'starter defaults' table (even approximate ranges) for chunk size, budget cap, and complexity thresholds directly in the plan or in a linked spec appendix. For triggered overlays, name at least the trigger conditions and overlay types so an executor can implement a concrete router rather than invent one. Mark items that are genuinely deferred to experiment results as explicit pre-requisite sub-tasks so the executor knows to block on them.

findings.location: Title, Context, Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria
Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling' bullet; 'Criteria registry' bullet; 'Attestation' bullet
findings.finding: The plan makes several assertions about the existing codebase that an executor must verify before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — the plan treats solidlsp as an existing, ready-to-use dependency; if it is present but not exposed as a library API, or if its interface differs from what the Layer-1 checks require, the file-resolution criterion will need rework. (2) 'reusing rebar.llm extension points' and 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — these extension points are assumed to already support the declarative-descriptor / generic-orchestrator pattern described; if they don't, significant new infrastructure is needed beyond what the plan scopes. (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' for attestation — the plan assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is sufficiently generic/composable to be extended for plan-review; if it is tightly coupled to completion-verification semantics, extension may require a rewrite rather than extension. (4) 'ast-grep (resolution/callers)' — assumed already available/integrated; unconfirmed. Cannot confirm or deny any of these without inspecting the live repo.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Integration prerequisites' checklist in the plan that an executor can tick off at start: (a) solidlsp is importable as a Python library with the API used in Layer-1; (b) ast-grep binary/wrapper is available; (c) rebar.llm exposes the catalog/prompt/runner interfaces in the way described; (d) close-gate signing is parameterisable. If any prerequisite fails, flag it as an early spike/task rather than discovering it mid-implementation.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Criteria registry' bullet; 'Observability / FP substrate' bullet; AC: 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator'
findings.finding: Two areas warrant a Rule-of-Three / NIH check that cannot be confirmed from plan text alone: (1) The 'declarative criteria registry + generic orchestrator' pattern is described as reusing rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner. If the existing rebar.llm runner already handles declarative descriptors generically, introducing a second registry/orchestrator layer would be duplication. Conversely, if rebar.llm's runner is not generic enough, the new orchestrator is justified — but the plan does not clarify which is the case. (2) The `REVIEW_RESULT` 'reducer-ignored sidecar event' is a new event type with special reducer handling. If rebar already has a mechanism for out-of-band/sidecar events (e.g., for the completion-verifier's signed event), adding a distinct sidecar pattern for this feature may be unnecessary; the existing pattern might be reusable. Neither is definitively over-engineering from the plan text alone, but both should be cross-checked against existing code before building new infrastructure.
findings.suggested_edit: Before implementation, confirm whether rebar.llm's existing runner can be driven by declarative criterion descriptors without a new orchestrator wrapper, and whether the existing signed-event/sidecar pattern from the close gate can be reused for REVIEW_RESULT with a new event type rather than new plumbing.

findings.location: AC bullets: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and 'Claiming a ticket … requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation'
findings.finding:
Most of the 'done' signals are structural (event emitted, attestation signed, config knob present) rather than behavioral (gate actually fires and blocks/passes correctly). Two specific gaps:
1. The AC for 'defaults grounded in experiment results' defers to linked experiment work — but no AC confirms those experiments will be completed and their outputs incorporated before this epic is closed. If the experiments are a separate deliverable, there is a reachability gap between 'implement the defaults' and 'know what the defaults should be'.
2. The integration AC for `claim` says 'fast signed-attestation check' but there is no AC specifying an observable end-to-end test: e.g., 'given an unsigned ticket, a claim attempt fails with error code X'. Without this, a reviewer or CI job cannot confirm the gate is live, only that a signing module exists.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC: 'An integration test (or recorded scenario) demonstrates that (a) a claim on a ticket with no attestation is blocked, (b) a claim with a valid attestation proceeds, and (c) a material edit after signing invalidates and re-blocks.' Also add an AC or a follow-on cross-reference clarifying that experiment-derived defaults are a prerequisite input — or explicitly call out that shipped defaults are provisional placeholders pending experiment completion.

findings.location: AC section (12 distinct bullets); Design summary ('Two layers', 'Criteria registry', 'Attestation', 'claim integration', 'Configurable chunking', 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar', 'Per-criterion config', 'Docs'); Follow-on section (F1–F12 listed as out-of-scope)
findings.finding: This epic is substantially oversized for a single unit of agentic work. The AC lists at minimum 12 independent deliverables spanning: (1) a polyglot Layer-1 deterministic engine with 7+ sub-checks; (2) a Layer-2 advisory LLM engine with 12+ criteria; (3) a criteria registry + generic orchestrator; (4) an attestation/signing subsystem; (5) `claim` command integration; (6) configurable chunking/routing/budget/escalation logic; (7) a `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer exemption; (8) per-criterion config knob; (9) bug-exemption routing; (10) experiment-grounded defaults (with the experiments themselves implicitly required); (11) full docs; (12) the coaching feedback spec conformance check. Each of these is a coherent vertical slice that could be built and tested independently. Packing all 12 into one session risks partial completion, large rollback surface, and compounded spec drift — the exact failure mode the gate itself is designed to prevent. The plan acknowledges an epic structure but does not enumerate any child tickets, making it unclear whether child decomposition is planned or whether this is expected to execute as a single leaf.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into at minimum: (A) Layer-1 deterministic floor + fail-open polyglot tooling; (B) Layer-2 advisory criteria engine + criteria registry/orchestrator; (C) Attestation subsystem + material-event-commit binding; (D) `claim` command integration + `--force` override; (E) `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar + reducer exemption; (F) Configurable routing/chunking/budget/escalation; (G) Docs + CI-check pattern. Each child AC should reference only its own deliverable. The current epic description is well-suited as the parent/container — add an explicit children section listing A–G.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard'; AC: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python'; 'Attestation … extends the close-gate signing'; '`claim` integration'
findings.finding:
The plan names the external/library dependencies (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) but does not name any internal rebar modules, files, or symbols that will be created or modified. Specific gaps that cannot be resolved without the codebase:
1. The `claim` command — its current module/file path, and what exactly changes in its hot path — is unnamed. Callers of `claim` that may need updating are unacknowledged.
2. 'Extends the close-gate signing' implies shared attestation infrastructure with the completion-verifier, but no file/symbol/interface names are given. If the close-gate signing module is not designed for extension, this could require refactoring that is not scoped.
3. The 'reducer-ignored sidecar' implies modifying the event reducer's dispatch table; that file is unnamed.
4. The 'criteria registry' and 'generic orchestrator' are described functionally but have no proposed module names or integration points with `rebar.llm`.
None of this is necessarily wrong — the plan may rely on the linked session log for file-level detail — but it creates ambiguity about blast radius and whether any consumers of the close-gate signing or the `claim` command need coordinated updates.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules touched' section (even a provisional list) naming: the `claim` command file, the close-gate attestation module being extended, the event reducer dispatch table, and the proposed new modules (criteria registry, layer-1 engine, layer-2 engine, sidecar emitter). Flag any callers of `claim` or the attestation module that will need review. Mark items as tentative if the session log is the authoritative source.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section pairs identified below
findings.finding:
Three tension points found; none are outright contradictions, but they are ambiguous enough to risk divergent implementation:

1. **Out-of-band review vs. blocking claim (Design summary vs. AC 'claim integration'):** The design says 'the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND' and 'claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation,' yet AC#1 says 'absence or a material-edit-invalidated signature BLOCKS the claim.' These are consistent only if the agent is expected to run the review tool separately before ever invoking `claim`. The plan does not describe how an agent is expected to trigger the out-of-band review, or what happens the very first time a ticket is claimed (no prior attestation exists). This workflow gap could lead to an agent being permanently blocked with no clear recovery path.

2. **Experiment-grounded defaults vs. in-scope delivery (AC bullet vs. Design summary):** AC says 'Defaults … grounded in the experiment results' and the design says 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments,' but experiments are not listed as a child deliverable or a prerequisite — they appear to be assumed complete. If experiments are follow-on (like F1–F12), the AC is unreachable within this epic.

3. **Bugs exempt (AC: 'Bugs are exempt') vs. routing description (Design: 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type'):** The routing pivot is explicitly 'NOT ticket type,' yet bug exemption IS a ticket-type-based route. This is a narrow but real contradiction in the routing logic that an implementer could resolve in conflicting ways.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Add a workflow description (even one sentence) in the plan explaining the expected agent flow: 'Agent runs `rebar review-plan <ticket>`, receives findings, revises, re-runs until attestation issued, then calls `claim`.' Clarify first-claim behavior (no prior attestation = blocked immediately, or a grace period?).
2. Explicitly state whether experiments are a prerequisite input or in-scope deliverable, and add a cross-reference or dependency link.
3. Reconcile the routing statement: change 'NOT ticket type' to 'NOT ticket type (except bug exemption, which is a ticket-type pre-filter applied before routing).'

findings.location: AC bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and Design summary: 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data; per-plan budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing'
findings.finding: The plan references an 'empirical' basis for chunking/budget/depth defaults ('default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments') and defers to a linked session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) and 'linked experiment work' for the concrete values. There is no AC or success criterion that states what the experiment outputs are, how they will be captured, or what 'done' looks like for that sub-task. If the experiment work is a follow-on (not in scope), the AC bullet referencing it creates a circular dependency: the epic cannot be 'done' until experiments exist whose outputs are not themselves defined in this plan. Additionally, there is no explicit definition of what constitutes a 'valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation' for the open→in_progress gate — the staleness boundary is described (material-event-commit binding, enumeration of what invalidates vs. does not) but the exact signing algorithm, key material / HMAC scheme, or verification contract that `claim` checks is left implicit. This makes 'done' for the attestation/claim integration hard to verify from the plan alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC bullet that defines what the experiment deliverables are and explicitly states whether they are in-scope for this epic or a prerequisite. If out-of-scope, move the 'grounded in experiment results' bullet to Follow-on work (F-series) or add a concrete fallback default. Additionally, add one AC bullet specifying the attestation format (e.g., 'Attestation is a HMAC-SHA256 over canonical ticket event fields, verified by `claim` against the stored public key') so 'done' for that deliverable is unambiguous.

findings.location: Entire plan: 12 AC bullets covering Layer-1 criteria, Layer-2 criteria, criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation subsystem, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget, feedback-authoring, REVIEW_RESULT event, per-criterion config, bug exemption, experiment-grounded defaults, and docs.
findings.finding: This ticket is labelled an 'epic' and does list follow-on work (F1–F12), but all 12 AC bullets remain in-scope for this single epic with no children tickets defined or referenced. The AC covers at minimum six distinct, independently-shippable vertical slices: (1) Layer-1 deterministic tooling (LSP, ast-grep, syft, scc/lizard integration), (2) Layer-2 LLM advisory criteria + orchestrator, (3) attestation/signing subsystem, (4) `claim` integration, (5) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip, and (6) config surface + docs. Each slice touches different modules, requires different skills, and has its own test surface. As a 'leaf' this would be far too large to execute coherently in one session. As a parent-epic it lacks any decomposition into child tickets — children are neither present in this plan nor referenced as already-filed linked tickets. The F1–F12 follow-ons are explicitly 'NOT part of this epic', so they do not substitute for children.
findings.suggested_edit: File child tickets for each independently-shippable vertical slice (suggested breakdown above) and add a 'Children' section to this epic listing their IDs. Each child should be scoped to one session's worth of coherent work (e.g., 'Layer-1 tooling harness', 'Layer-2 criteria registry + orchestrator', 'Attestation + claim integration', 'REVIEW_RESULT event + reducer', 'Config + defaults + docs'). The epic's own ACs should then be satisfied when all children are complete, not by direct implementation.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers); syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps); scc + lizard (size)' and AC bullet: 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open: readiness shape … file/symbol/import resolution (LSP/ast-grep), package existence (syft + registry + fallback), oversize signals, task-DAG validity, AC/DD quality, destructive sniff.'
findings.finding: The plan names concrete tools (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) and states they are 'already in repo' or assumed available, but no specific source files, modules, or integration points within the rebar codebase are identified. The blast radius of the new gate on the existing `claim` command path, the completion-verifier module (which is explicitly cited as a design analog), and rebar.llm's 'catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' extension points is acknowledged at a high level ('reusing rebar.llm extension points') but not mapped to concrete file paths or symbol names. This makes it impossible to verify from the plan alone whether the named extension points actually exist and accept the new criteria-registry pattern, or whether callers of `claim` that currently assume no attestation requirement will break silently. These questions require codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / Symbols Touched' section (even a best-effort list) naming: the existing `claim` command module, the completion-verifier module being mirrored, the rebar.llm extension-point entry file, and the proposed new module tree (e.g., `rebar/plan_review/layer1/`, `rebar/plan_review/layer2/`, `rebar/plan_review/attestation.py`). Note explicitly which callers of `claim` will need updates. Mark anything speculative as 'TBC after codebase audit'.

findings.location: Multiple sections: (A) Design summary 'claim is a FAST check … heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND' vs. AC 'Claiming a ticket (open→in_progress) requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation; absence … blocks the claim'; (B) Design summary 'LAYER 1 … BLOCKS' vs. AC 'Layer-2 … judgment criteria are advisory by default' vs. AC 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override'; (C) Design summary 'Sound checks block; judgment is advisory; AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' vs. AC 'INDETERMINATE is non-blocking by default (project-configurable)'
findings.finding:
Three potential internal tensions are present, though each has a plausible reconciling reading:

(A) The design says the heavy review runs 'out-of-band' yet the success criterion says 'absence of a signature blocks the claim'. It is not stated whether the out-of-band run must complete before the very first claim attempt is valid, or whether the agent can start work and the gate fires asynchronously. If truly out-of-band, a race condition exists where an agent could claim while a review is in flight. The plan likely intends 'out-of-band relative to the claim fast-path' (pre-run, then claim checks the cached sig), but this is implicit.

(B) The design says 'A REVIEW PROCESS, not a dialog — the client revises and re-runs to earn a fresh signature' (implying synchronous revision loops), while 'out-of-band' implies asynchronous. These two characterizations are mildly in tension but may refer to different phases.

(C) 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' in the design but the AC only mentions INDETERMINATE as a configurable non-blocking outcome; no AC covers the adjudicator pathway or what 'escalation' means operationally (a separate tool call? a human? a stronger model auto-retry?). This gap could leave the AMBIGUOUS verdict handling unimplemented.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Sequencing / flow' paragraph (or a numbered step diagram) clarifying: (1) the out-of-band review must complete and produce a signature BEFORE `claim` is invoked — `claim` only checks the cached signature; (2) what 'escalation' of an AMBIGUOUS verdict means operationally (e.g., 'retry with a stronger model tier; if still AMBIGUOUS, treat as INDETERMINATE'). This would resolve all three tensions without altering the design intent.

findings.location: AC bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment results)'
findings.finding: Several termination/verification conditions depend on artifacts that are not yet in-scope or are deferred: (1) The AC requires defaults to be 'grounded in the experiment results' but those experiments are referenced only by a session-log ID ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b') that an executing agent may not have access to — if those results don't exist yet, the AC is un-closeable. (2) The FP/remediation tracking that would confirm 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criterion 3) is explicitly deferred to F1/F2, so that success criterion cannot be verified within this epic. (3) There is no explicit acceptance test or integration smoke-test described for the end-to-end path: trigger → Layer-1 → Layer-2 → attestation signed → claim unblocked. The 'done' signal is implicitly 'all AC checkboxes pass' but the plan gives no concrete verification procedure for the hardest items (e.g. how is coaching quality evaluated before merge?).
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC bullet that explicitly names the integration test scenario covering the full open→claim path. Clarify whether the experiment results are a prerequisite (filed separately and linked) or whether sensible hardcoded defaults are acceptable until experiments are run. For the 'measurably help' success criterion, either scope a minimal proxy metric (e.g. 'finding cites file+line') achievable in this epic, or move the 'measurably' qualifier to the F1 follow-on.

findings.location: AC section (12 bullets); Design summary ('Two layers', 'Criteria registry', 'Attestation', 'claim integration', 'Configurable chunking', 'REVIEW_RESULT', 'Polyglot tooling'); Follow-on list (12 items F1–F12)
findings.finding: This epic bundles at minimum six distinct, independently shippable vertical slices into a single ticket with no child tickets yet filed: (1) Layer-1 deterministic criteria + polyglot tooling harness, (2) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching-feedback authoring, (3) Criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (4) Attestation model + signing + material-edit invalidation, (5) `claim` integration + `--force` override, (6) `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip + fingerprints. Each of these has non-trivial design and implementation work. The plan acknowledges this is an 'epic' but does NOT decompose it into child tickets (no children are listed; the follow-on work is explicitly deferred as F1–F12). An agent attempting to execute this in one session will almost certainly exceed any reasonable context/session budget, and the AC's own item 11 ('Defaults… grounded in experiment results') presupposes a separate experiment workstream. The plan explicitly flags the epic framing but provides no decomposition plan for how the epic's children will be structured.
findings.suggested_edit: File child tickets for at minimum the six slices above before or immediately as part of this epic's first action. Suggested vertical slices: (a) Attestation model + signing + material-event-commit binding; (b) `claim` fast-check integration + `--force` override; (c) Layer-1 deterministic criteria (each tool group could be a further child: LSP/ast-grep, syft/registry, scc/lizard, structural readiness); (d) Criteria registry + generic orchestrator; (e) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching spec; (f) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event. This epic's own AC should require those children to exist and be linked before the epic is considered in-progress.

findings.location: Design summary: 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep … syft + registry … scc + lizard'; AC bullet 1 (Layer-1 criteria); AC bullet 6 ('centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time')
findings.finding: The plan names several concrete tools and integration points (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, lizard, scc, rebar.llm extension points, the existing completion-verifier signing logic, the reducer) but does not name the specific files/modules/classes that will be created or modified. In particular: (1) The 'extends the close-gate signing' note in AC bullet 4 implies changes to an existing signing module — the name of that module is not given and needs codebase confirmation. (2) 'Reusing rebar.llm extension points' implies a specific catalog/contracts/prompt/runner interface that is not described; whether it is sufficient for the new orchestrator is unverifiable without the codebase. (3) The blast radius of adding a new gate at open→in_progress on the `claim` command is unacknowledged with respect to existing tests for that command path — no mention of test updates for the claim path. These are all AMBIGUOUS (not necessarily wrong) because the plan explicitly defers file-level detail to child tickets, but the epic-level plan should at minimum enumerate the modules touched.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files / modules in scope' subsection to the epic listing: (a) the existing signing module to be extended; (b) the claim-command handler; (c) the reducer event-dispatch table (for REVIEW_RESULT skip); (d) the rebar.llm orchestration entry points. Even approximate module paths remove ambiguity for child-ticket authors.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section pairs identified below
findings.finding:
Three potential internal tensions were found; none are clear-cut contradictions but each is worth resolving before child tickets are written:

1. **'BLOCKS' vs advisory posture (Design summary ↔ AC bullet 1):** The Design summary says Layer-1 'BLOCKS (frozen, unambiguous, cheap … fail-open)' and Layer-2 'SURFACES findings … never blocks by default.' AC bullet 9 says 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override.' These are consistent in spirit, but the plan simultaneously says Layer-1 checks are 'blocking by construction' AND that there is a per-criterion knob that makes any criterion advisory. It is unclear whether Layer-1 criteria can be demoted to advisory via the per-criterion knob or whether they are permanently blocking. This ambiguity could cause the implementation to diverge.

2. **Experiment-grounded defaults vs. in-scope deliverable (AC bullet 11 ↔ Follow-on F1/F10):** AC bullet 11 requires 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results.' Follow-ons F1 (FP/remediation-rate tracking) and F10 (calibrated abstention + labeled gold set) are explicitly out of scope. If the experiments required for bullet 11 are also deferred, the AC is internally unreachable within the epic. If the experiments are in-scope, they are not listed in any AC bullet.

3. **INDETERMINATE non-blocking default ↔ blocking claim gate (Design summary ↔ Success Criterion 1):** The design says INDETERMINATE is 'non-blocking by default (project-configurable).' Success Criterion 1 says 'absence or a material-edit-invalidated signature blocks the claim.' It is unclear what happens when the review exits INDETERMINATE (budget exhausted, tool unavailable): does the claim block (no valid signature) or pass (non-blocking INDETERMINATE)? The plan implies the out-of-band review must complete before claim is possible, but never explicitly resolves what a timed-out or budget-exhausted review produces.
findings.suggested_edit: Resolve the three tensions explicitly: (1) State whether Layer-1 criteria are exempt from the per-criterion advisory demotion knob. (2) Either add an AC bullet for the required experiments or clarify that 'experiment results' refers to pre-existing data and link it explicitly. (3) Add a sentence to the attestation section: 'An INDETERMINATE review result produces a [partial/absent] attestation that [blocks/does not block] claim by default.'

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: One acceptance criterion explicitly depends on experiment results from a session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) that is referenced but not included in this plan. An executing agent must locate and interpret those results to satisfy this criterion, which represents an out-of-session dependency if that log is not accessible in the working environment. Additionally, AC 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented' covers ~13 distinct sub-criteria and a broad pass, plus triggered overlays — the aggregate scope is very large for a single epic with no decomposition into child tickets shown here. Whether this fits in one working session depends heavily on how 'implemented' is scoped (stub vs. production-ready). The plan notes bugs are exempt and follow-ons are deferred, but the remaining scope is still substantial.
findings.suggested_edit: Either include a summary of the experiment results directly in the plan body (or an appendix), or replace the criterion with 'Defaults chosen and documented with rationale' so an agent can satisfy it without the external session log. Consider adding a note on the expected fidelity level (e.g., 'working end-to-end prototype' vs. 'production-hardened') to clarify what 'implemented' means for the Layer-2 criteria.

findings.location: Design summary: 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator'; Verdict model section vs. AC list
findings.finding: Two potential gaps between description and ACs: (1) The design summary describes AMBIGUOUS verdict escalation to a 'higher-tier adjudicator', but no AC specifies how that adjudicator is invoked, what it returns, or how its result is surfaced — this described behavior has no corresponding acceptance criterion. (2) The design summary mentions 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' as a routing signal; the AC for escalation includes centrality, but there is no AC that covers how centrality is computed or what data source is used. These are described mechanisms without a measurable criterion to verify them. No outright duplicates detected across the AC list. Terminology is mostly consistent; 'judgment criterion' (design summary) vs. 'advisory criteria' (ACs) is used interchangeably in a way that is clear in context but slightly inconsistent.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a criterion covering AMBIGUOUS escalation: e.g., 'When a criterion returns AMBIGUOUS, the system routes to a higher-tier model/adjudicator and records the outcome in REVIEW_RESULT.' Add a criterion or sub-point specifying how centrality/blast-radius is computed (e.g., 'Centrality is derived from LSP call-graph data at plan time and attached to the review context'). Standardize 'judgment criterion' → 'advisory criterion' throughout.

findings.location: Context section: 'when an agent claims a ticket (open→in_progress)...' and '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'
Title vs. Design summary, Success Criteria, and AC list
findings.finding: The title 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)' is faithfully served throughout. The design summary, success criteria, and ACs all consistently describe a gate at the open→in_progress transition, a two-layer review, coaching posture, signed attestation, and `claim` integration. There is no scope drift into unrelated features; the follow-on list explicitly carves out items (bug tickets, FP tooling, cross-ticket analysis) that are excluded. No contradiction detected between headline intent and detailed steps.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — no AC addresses testing; Success Criteria section — no criterion addresses testing
findings.finding: The plan describes a substantial, multi-component feature (Layer-1 deterministic checks, Layer-2 LLM-based review, attestation signing, `claim` integration, config system, sidecar events, criteria registry/orchestrator) but contains NO acceptance criterion and NO success criterion that specifies how any of these components will be tested. There is no mention of unit tests for the deterministic Layer-1 checks, integration tests for the `claim` gate, contract/behavioral tests for the criteria orchestrator, red/green ordering, failure-path coverage (e.g., what happens when solidlsp crashes, when the LLM times out, when the budget is exhausted), or boundary cases (e.g., a ticket with no ACs, a ticket with all AMBIGUOUS verdicts). The fail-open behavior ('unsupported lang → skipped = PASS') is a critical correctness property with no stated test.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing AC, for example: 'Unit tests cover each Layer-1 deterministic check including fail-open paths (unsupported language, tool crash, timeout → PASS). Integration tests cover the full open→in_progress gate: valid attestation passes, absent attestation blocks, stale attestation blocks, --force override is recorded. Behavioral tests cover the criteria orchestrator: per-criterion PASS/AMBIGUOUS/FAIL, INDETERMINATE on budget exhaustion, chunk-atomic checkpointing. At least one red-before-green test demonstrates a flawed plan is caught and a revised plan passes.'

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified assertions about existing infrastructure: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — assumed available and usable for file/symbol/import resolution; (2) 'reusing rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — assumes these extension points exist, are stable, and are sufficient to host the criteria orchestrator without modification; (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes the completion-verifier's signing mechanism is factored in a way that supports extension; (4) 'ast-grep (resolution/callers)' — assumed installed and callable. If any of these assumptions are wrong, the implementation path changes materially. The plan cites the session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` as the design-of-record, but an executing agent needs these assumptions verified in the actual codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Verified assumptions' section listing: solidlsp integration point and API surface confirmed; rebar.llm extension points confirmed sufficient; close-gate signing code confirmed extensible; ast-grep confirmed available. Alternatively, add an AC: 'A spike confirms solidlsp, ast-grep, rebar.llm orchestration hooks, and close-gate signing are usable as described, with any gaps noted.'

findings.location: AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: ... intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)'
findings.finding: Several acceptance criteria depend on external, out-of-band work that may not be completable within a single session: (1) The AC for 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' references a separate experiment work item that must be completed first — if those experiments have not yet been run, the defaults cannot be set, blocking the AC. (2) 'intent fidelity (2-step blind-restate)' implies a calibration/validation process that may require iterative data collection beyond one session. (3) AC 'Docs: how the gate works...' is listed but no scope or definition-of-done for the docs is given, making it potentially open-ended. (4) The linked session log `63cc-ec94-4792-464b` is cited as holding 'full design detail' — if that log is unavailable to the executor, several criteria become ambiguous in scope.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) include the experiment results directly in the plan (or assert they are already complete and cite the output), or (b) split the 'defaults grounded in experiments' AC into a follow-on ticket. For docs, specify the minimum required sections and target audience so an executor knows when 'done' is reached.

findings.location: Plan body: 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type' vs. AC: 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing (complexity + has_children); escalation by size+complexity+centrality'; also Follow-on F6: 'bug-ticket review (dedicated epic)' vs. AC: 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)'
findings.finding: Overall coherence between description and ACs is strong, but a few gaps/ambiguities exist: (1) The plan body describes a 'bounded broad open-ended pass' as part of Layer-2 but the AC for Layer-2 criteria lists named criteria and then 'a bounded broad pass' without specifying what bounds it — the criterion and description are loosely matched. (2) The plan body mentions 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' in the design summary, but the AC references it only in passing ('centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time') — no AC explicitly covers the implementation of centrality computation as a deliverable. (3) Terminology: 'INDETERMINATE' is introduced in the verdict model but the AC uses both 'INDETERMINATE outcome' and 'non-blocking default' without a dedicated AC that formally defines or tests this verdict type. (4) No orphaned criteria were found; Follow-on items are correctly scoped out.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC covering centrality/blast-radius computation as a deliverable (not just a routing input). Clarify what 'bounded' means for the broad pass (e.g., 'one LLM call of at most N tokens'). Add a one-line definition of INDETERMINATE to the design summary for consistency.

findings.location: Context section: 'when an agent claims a ticket (open→in_progress), nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound before the agent executes it. Flawed plans... get executed anyway, and early errors compound over autonomous agent trajectories (~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects).'
Title: 'Plan-review verification gate (open→in_progress)'; Design summary, Success Criteria, Acceptance Criteria, Follow-on work sections
findings.finding: The plan's body faithfully serves the title. The design, success criteria, and ACs all orient around the open→in_progress gate. Follow-on items are correctly separated. No scope drift detected — bugs are explicitly excluded with a rationale, and the CI/rigor signal is scoped as a composable output rather than a standalone feature. No contradictions found between headline intent and detailed steps.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 12 AC bullets); Success Criteria section
findings.finding: The plan contains no testing plan. There is no mention of: (1) how any of the implemented criteria, orchestrator, attestation signing, claim integration, sidecar event emission, or config surface will be tested; (2) what test types will be used (unit, integration, end-to-end); (3) failure-path coverage (e.g., what happens when Layer-1 tooling crashes, when a signing key is absent, when a budget is exhausted mid-chunk, when `--force` is used); (4) RED-before-GREEN ordering or any TDD posture; (5) boundary/edge cases (e.g., a ticket with no AC section, a ticket with zero children that has high complexity). The plan references 'experiments' for calibrating defaults but does not describe those experiments as tests of the gate's behavior. The completion-verifier is cited as a model but no mention of reusing or extending its test suite.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Testing approach' section (or AC) specifying: (a) unit tests for each Layer-1 deterministic check with fail-open cases; (b) integration tests for the claim→attestation→gate path including --force override; (c) contract/behavioral tests for the sidecar REVIEW_RESULT event schema; (d) at least one end-to-end scenario per routing branch (bug-exempt, has_children, high-complexity); (e) explicit coverage of the 'tool unavailable → skipped = PASS' fail-open path. Reference the completion-verifier test suite as a pattern to mirror.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep (resolution/callers)'; 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'extends the close-gate signing'; 'mirrors completion-verifier'
findings.finding: The plan makes several unverified assumptions about the existing codebase that an executor would need to confirm before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — assumed to be present and functional; if it is absent or broken, Layer-1 LSP checks cannot be implemented as described. (2) 'reusing rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — assumes these extension points exist and are compatible with the criteria-registry pattern; no AC verifies this compatibility. (3) 'extends the close-gate signing' — assumes the close-gate signing mechanism is modular/extensible enough to be extended rather than duplicated; no verification step is called out. (4) 'mirrors completion-verifier' — assumes the completion-verifier's architecture is a suitable template; if it is monolithic or tightly coupled, this assumption could require significant rework. These are plausible assumptions for an existing repo but cannot be confirmed from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Prerequisites / verified assumptions' checklist to the plan that an executor must confirm before starting: (a) solidlsp present and LSP server startable for at least one supported language; (b) rebar.llm runner accepts external criteria descriptors; (c) close-gate signing is exposed as a reusable utility. This converts implicit assumptions into explicit pre-conditions.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Execution' bullet: 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB … default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Several design decisions are deferred to experiment results that have not been recorded in the plan itself. Specifically: (1) default chunking size, (2) budget caps, and (3) escalation thresholds (size+complexity+centrality) are all described as 'empirically set' or 'grounded in experiment results' but the actual values — or even the parameter ranges — are not stated. AC item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' simply references a session log ID ('63cc-ec94-4792-464b'), which an executing agent may not be able to access. An agent implementing this plan will have to either invent values or halt to ask, which violates the no-clarification-needed expectation. The 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' in AC Layer-2 are also not further defined — what triggers them, and which overlays exist, is not specified.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the empirically derived defaults (even as provisional numbers subject to revision) in the plan body, or add a concrete AC item that names the experiment ticket/artifact where those numbers live and confirms they are accessible to the implementing agent. Similarly, enumerate at least the categories of 'triggered overlays' so an agent knows the full set to implement.

findings.location: Success Criteria items 1–6 vs. Acceptance Criteria full list; 'Follow-on' section items F1–F12
findings.finding: The success criteria and ACs are thorough and largely form a verifiable checklist. However, two gaps exist: (1) AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' — the verification method is unclear because the referenced experiment results live in a session log not guaranteed to be machine-readable; a reviewer could not confirm 'groundedness' without human judgment. (2) The plan explicitly moves FP/remediation tracking (F1) and CI integration helpers (F11) to follow-on, but AC item 6 ('The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process') is listed as in-scope. The line between in-scope CI checkability and follow-on F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers') is blurry — an agent could reasonably implement either a stub or a full CI integration and still claim done. Termination is otherwise well-defined for the other ACs.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify AC 6 with a concrete, minimal definition of 'checkable by an external CI process' (e.g., 'a CLI exit-code check on attestation presence/validity is sufficient; full CI helpers are F11'). Also add a note on how 'grounded in experiment results' will be verified (e.g., a specific document or ticket number).

findings.location: Full plan — labeled 'epic'; no child tickets listed; AC list spans ~12 distinct subsystems
findings.finding: This is explicitly an epic, but the plan contains no decomposition into child tickets. The AC list alone spans at least 10–12 distinct implementation units: Layer-1 deterministic criteria (8 sub-checks), Layer-2 advisory criteria (11+ sub-checks), the criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing + binding, `claim` integration, configurable chunking/routing/escalation, feedback-authoring conformance check, `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event + reducer skip, per-criterion config knob, bug exemption routing, experiment-grounded defaults, and documentation. Each of these is a meaningful unit of work on its own. The plan names 'Follow-on' tickets (F1–F12) but gives zero decomposition of the in-scope work. An agent handed this epic as a single unit of work has no safe stopping point and no coherent one-session scope.
findings.suggested_edit: File child tickets (at minimum): (a) Layer-1 structural floor, (b) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching spec, (c) criteria registry + orchestrator, (d) attestation + material-event binding, (e) `claim` integration + `--force` override, (f) configurable chunking/routing/budget/escalation, (g) `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event, (h) docs. The epic description is excellent as a parent spec; it just needs its children created and linked.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling' bullet: 'solidlsp (already in repo) + ast-grep … syft + registry + LLM-fallback … scc + lizard'
findings.finding: The tooling choices (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard) all appear purposefully selected and are described as already-present or well-justified. The criteria registry + generic orchestrator reuses 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', which is a deliberate reuse-not-reinvent choice — good. One mild concern: the plan introduces a new 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event' type that is reducer-ignored. Without codebase access it's impossible to confirm whether an existing event pattern (e.g., the completion-verifier's result event) could be extended rather than creating a new type. This is not necessarily over-engineering, but warrants a check.
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm (in plan text or a note) that no existing event type (e.g., completion-verifier result events) can be reused or extended for REVIEW_RESULT, and that a new type is genuinely required.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'; Design summary — 'Trigger / enforcement' bullet; no file paths named anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan names zero concrete file paths, module names, or symbol names anywhere. The `claim` command is mentioned as the integration point, but its current location in the codebase is not specified. Similarly, 'extends the close-gate signing' implies the attestation module touches the existing completion-verifier signing code, but no file or symbol is cited. For an epic this level of abstraction is partially expected, but the absence of any file-level guidance means an implementing agent must discover the entire edit surface from scratch, raising the risk of missed consumers (e.g., other places that check ticket state on open→in_progress transitions). Blast radius is not acknowledged beyond the `claim` entry point.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a section (even brief) naming: the module/file where `claim` lives, the completion-verifier signing code to be extended, and any known callers of the open→in_progress transition that will need updating. Acknowledge that a full call-graph analysis is deferred to child tickets.

findings.location: Design summary – 'Execution' bullet: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; AC bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding: The plan defers a concrete implementation decision — what the default chunking/budget/depth values actually are — to external experiment results that are not reproduced here. The linked session log (`63cc-ec94-4792-464b`) and 'linked experiment work' are not accessible to a reviewer or executor who only has this ticket. If those experiments have already run and the results are settled, the executing agent should be able to proceed; if they have not, the agent must stop and figure out the values. The plan does not state which case is true. Similarly, 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' leaves the escalation thresholds unspecified. These are real design numbers an executor needs to write code.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) inline the experiment-derived defaults (e.g., 'default chunk size = N criteria per call; default budget cap = X tokens; depth thresholds = [S, M, L]') into the AC or an appendix, OR (b) explicitly call out a child ticket/spike to settle these values before the implementation ticket is started, making the dependency explicit.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria sections
Full plan — entire AC list; Follow-on section listing F1–F12; no child-ticket decomposition present.
findings.finding: This is explicitly styled as an 'epic' but contains no child tickets. The AC list alone enumerates at least 12 independently deliverable work items (Layer-1 criteria impl, Layer-2 criteria impl, criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation + signing, `claim` integration, configurable chunking/routing/escalation, feedback-authoring conformance, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event, per-criterion config knob, bug exemption routing, experiment-grounded defaults, docs). These span multiple subsystems (LSP tooling, LLM orchestration, event store, CLI, config, docs) and cannot coherently be executed in one agent session. The plan mentions that routing 'pivots on has_children + complexity' for its own review depth, yet the ticket itself has no children. The follow-on section (F1–F12) correctly defers some items, but the in-scope work is still far too large for one unit of work.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into child tickets at minimum along these vertical slices: (1) Layer-1 deterministic floor checks + fail-open tooling harness; (2) criteria registry + generic orchestrator reusing rebar.llm; (3) Layer-2 advisory review + coaching-spec feedback authoring; (4) attestation signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation; (5) `claim` integration + `--force` override; (6) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip; (7) configurable chunking/budget/depth/escalation (after experiments); (8) docs. Link all as children of this epic.

findings.location: Design summary – 'Criteria registry' bullet: 'each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'; 'Polyglot tooling' bullet listing solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly directs reuse of rebar.llm's existing catalog/contracts/prompt/runner infrastructure, which is the right instinct. However, without codebase access it is ambiguous whether the 'criteria registry' and 'generic orchestrator' constitute a new abstraction layer or are thin wrappers around already-existing extension points. If rebar.llm already has a mechanism for registering and running named criteria (as is implied), adding a separate 'criteria registry' could be redundant. Likewise, the tooling stack (solidlsp already in repo + ast-grep + syft + scc + lizard) is five tools; it is unclear whether lighter-weight alternatives already present in the repo could cover the same surface. This cannot be confirmed without the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief note in the plan stating what specifically rebar.llm already provides vs. what net-new abstractions are being created, to allow the executor to stay DRY without re-investigating.

findings.location: Design summary and AC – no specific file paths or module names named; 'extends the close-gate signing'; '`claim` integration'.
findings.finding: The plan names no concrete file paths, module names, or symbol names. It references the 'close-gate signing' as an existing artifact to extend, the `claim` command as an integration point, the `rebar.llm` runner as a reuse point, and the event store's reducer as something to modify (skip REVIEW_RESULT). Without codebase access, it is impossible to verify that these touchpoints exist at the named locations or that no unnamed callers of the `claim` flow would be broken by a new blocking check. The plan does acknowledge blast-radius conceptually ('centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time') but does not enumerate which modules in the existing codebase are in scope for modification.
findings.suggested_edit: In at least one child ticket (e.g., the `claim` integration child), name the specific files/modules/functions that will change (e.g., 'modify `rebar/claim.py::claim_ticket()` to call `verify_plan_attestation()`') so blast-radius is concrete rather than conceptual.

findings.location: Design summary – 'Trigger/enforcement' bullet vs. AC bullet on 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'.
findings.finding: Two internal tensions exist: (1) The design summary states the heavy review runs 'OUT-OF-BAND' and that `claim` is a 'FAST check for a valid signed attestation'. The AC then requires that 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth [be] grounded in the experiment results' — but those experiments are listed as prerequisite external work with no ticket filed (they are not in the Follow-on section either, which covers post-epic work). This means a required input to implementation (the empirical defaults) has no clear owner or sequencing. (2) The design says 'INDETERMINATE is non-blocking by default (project-configurable)' while the AC says 'per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing and an INDETERMINATE outcome (non-blocking default, configurable)' — these are consistent — but neither section addresses what the `claim` fast-check does when the out-of-band review has not yet produced a result (i.e., the attestation simply doesn't exist yet). The Success Criteria says 'absence … blocks the claim', implying the agent must wait for the out-of-band review to finish before it can claim. This sequencing is left implicit and could confuse an executor.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Add the experiment work as an explicit prerequisite ticket referenced from this epic (not buried in AC prose). (2) Add a sentence to the 'Trigger/enforcement' section clarifying the workflow when no attestation yet exists — e.g., 'Agent triggers the out-of-band review, awaits completion, then retries claim' — so the executor doesn't have to infer it.

findings.location: NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT paragraph + SUCCESS CRITERIA section
SC5 ('behavior-bearing surfaces … removed from the review-gate allowlist') vs. title ('ruleset-enforced fail-closed review gate on the two-tier flow')
findings.finding: The title promises a focused hardening of the review gate, but SC5 introduces a non-trivial allowlist reclassification exercise (docs/**, skills, behavior-driving config become reviewable; images/lockfiles remain exempt). This is an additional scope item — classifying 'behavior-bearing' vs 'inert' surfaces requires content decisions that are meaningfully separate from wiring the gate. It is plausible this belongs here (you cannot truly 'enforce' without correct allowlisting), but the plan does not explain why the reclassification is bundled rather than being a prerequisite or follow-on, creating a mild risk of scope creep or of the gate landing with an incomplete/wrong allowlist.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence in the SC5 rationale explaining why allowlist reclassification is part of this epic rather than a gating prerequisite (e.g., 'SC5 is required to prevent the gate being vacuously bypassed by miscategorisation') to make intent fidelity explicit.

findings.location: SEQUENCING paragraph: 'stories 1-4 may proceed in parallel … story 7 (enforce-flip) depends on 1-6 ALL verified; story 8 (UX) is independent'
SC2 ('all review-enforcing CI wrappers fail-closed … in ALL THREE carriers — review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml') and SC3 ('R8 live-parity guard')
findings.finding: The plan mandates fail-closed token/precondition logic replicated across three distinct YAML workflow files plus an R8 live-parity guard, which is four separate enforcement surfaces for what is ultimately one policy check ('token must be valid, gate must be present'). Without access to the codebase it cannot be confirmed whether these files already have shared infrastructure (e.g., a composite action or reusable workflow) that would prevent duplication. If the fail-closed logic is copy-pasted into each file independently, this violates the Rule-of-Three and introduces drift risk. Conversely, if the plan intends to centralise this via a shared action (not stated), the plan should say so.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly state whether the fail-closed precondition logic is extracted into a single composite/reusable GitHub Actions step shared by all three carriers, or justify why per-file duplication is intentional (e.g., 'each carrier owns its own precondition check to avoid coupling').

findings.location: Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' and AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Two decision-deferral placeholders require external inputs that the plan does not self-contain: (1) 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB... default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' — the experiment results are not included, so an executor must go find them or choose arbitrary defaults; (2) the AC references 'linked experiment work' that is neither attached nor summarised. The executor cannot set the defaults without that data. Additionally, 'escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality' is described without defining the concrete thresholds (what complexity score triggers escalation? which 'stronger model'?), leaving real design decisions to the implementer.
findings.suggested_edit: Inline the experiment-derived defaults (e.g. 'default chunk size = N criteria per call, budget cap = X tokens, depth levels = low/medium/high mapped to complexity scores Y and Z') directly into the AC or a 'Defaults' subsection. Similarly specify the escalation thresholds numerically or reference a concrete lookup table rather than 'by size + complexity + centrality'.

findings.location: Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria sections; Follow-on work F1, F2, F4, F10, F11
findings.finding: The plan's own stated end-state is sound at a high level (each AC maps to a deliverable), but several loop-closing elements are deferred to follow-on tickets in ways that create a reachability gap: (a) The attestation is described as a 'composable CI rigor signal' and AC[6] says 'checkable by an external CI process', but F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers') is explicitly out of scope — so the CI-check pattern mentioned in Docs AC cannot be fully verified from within this epic. (b) FP/remediation-rate analysis (F1) is deferred, yet the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event is explicitly designed to feed it; the executor cannot confirm the schema is 'offline-analysis-ready' without knowing what the offline analysis will need. These are not blocking gaps (the plan has a benign reading: emit the event, defer analysis), but they mean two ACs ('CI-check pattern' and 'offline-analysis-ready') cannot be fully closed within this epic's scope.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) narrow the Docs AC to 'document how to inspect the signed attestation field' rather than 'the CI-check pattern' (leaving the full CI helper to F11), and annotate the REVIEW_RESULT schema note with 'schema frozen in this epic; analysis tooling in F1', OR (b) include a minimal stub CI-check script and a documented REVIEW_RESULT schema as in-scope deliverables.

findings.location: Full plan (description + AC list); 'Follow-on work' section listing F1–F12
findings.finding: This is an epic that explicitly names 12 follow-on tickets but provides no decomposition into child tickets within the epic itself. The AC list alone spans: a two-layer criteria engine, a criteria registry + generic orchestrator, attestation/signing, `claim` integration, configurable routing/escalation, budget/checkpointing, a REVIEW_RESULT event + reducer skip, per-criterion config, coaching-spec feedback authoring, polyglot tooling integration (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard), experiment-derived defaults, and docs. This is many independent vertical slices compressed into one ticket that would be very difficult to execute coherently in one session or even one sprint. The plan acknowledges it is an epic but does not break it into child tickets — meaning an agent handed this ticket must either execute the entire thing or self-decompose, neither of which matches rebar's own claim-gate design (which routes on 'has_children + complexity').
findings.suggested_edit: File child tickets covering natural vertical slices, for example: (1) Layer-1 structural floor + fail-open tooling, (2) criteria registry + orchestrator, (3) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching spec, (4) attestation + material-event binding + `claim` integration, (5) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event + reducer skip, (6) configurable routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, (7) docs. The epic ticket itself should then list these children and act only as the coordination parent.

findings.location: Design summary: 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored SIDECAR event'; 'Criteria registry: each criterion is a declarative descriptor; a generic orchestrator'; 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding'
findings.finding: Three areas warrant a NIH / over-engineering check against the live codebase, which cannot be confirmed from plan text alone: (1) The 'generic orchestrator' that runs declarative criterion descriptors appears to reuse 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' — if those extension points already exist this is fine reuse, but if they need to be created for this epic that is additional scope not surfaced in the AC. (2) The 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event' introduces a new event type with a reducer-skip rule; if rebar already has a pattern for reducer-ignored events (e.g. for the completion-verifier) this is consistent, but if it is novel it adds framework surface area. (3) Signing/attestation is described as 'extends the close-gate signing' — confirming that the close-gate signing mechanism is reusable and not being reimplemented is needed. None of these are definitively over-engineered from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence each confirming: (a) which existing rebar.llm extension points are reused verbatim vs. extended, (b) whether reducer-ignored event types already exist in the event model, and (c) that the close-gate signing module is imported/extended rather than re-implemented.

findings.location: AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'; Design summary: 'Bind to the latest MATERIAL event commit'; 'polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD): solidlsp... ast-grep... syft... scc... lizard'
findings.finding: The plan names the modules/tools it will integrate (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard, the `claim` command) and calls out the close-gate attestation as the extension point, which is a reasonable blast-radius acknowledgement. However, three specific edit targets cannot be confirmed from plan text: (1) whether `claim` is a single entry-point or spread across callers that all need updating; (2) whether the 'material event commit' binding logic touches the event reducer, the event store, or a separate signing module; (3) whether solidlsp and ast-grep are already wired into rebar's tool infrastructure or need new adapter code. These are AMBIGUOUS against the live codebase rather than hallucinated.
findings.suggested_edit: Name the specific files/modules to be modified for `claim` integration and attestation binding (e.g. 'modify `rebar/commands/claim.py` lines N–M to call `attestation.verify()`'), and clarify whether solidlsp/ast-grep adapters already exist or are new scope.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section pairs (detailed below)
findings.finding:
Three internal contradictions found:

1. **Routing logic vs. AC scope**: The Design summary states 'routing pivots on has_children + complexity, NOT ticket type' and 'Bugs are SKIPPED in this epic'. The AC then adds 'Bugs are exempt from this gate (routed to a follow-on)'. These are consistent in intent but the AC implies the bug-exemption logic IS implemented (a routing branch that detects bug-type and skips), while the Design summary says bugs get a 'dedicated follow-on'. If the bug-type routing branch is not implemented in this epic, the AC bullet is misleading; if it is implemented, the Design summary is misleading.

2. **INDETERMINATE non-blocking default vs. project-configurable**: The Design summary says 'INDETERMINATE is non-blocking by default (project-configurable)' and the Verdict model section confirms this. The AC says 'per-plan budget cap with chunk-atomic checkpointing and an INDETERMINATE outcome (non-blocking default, configurable)'. Both are consistent, but the Success Criteria bullet 4 ('A project can configure risk posture, chunking, budget caps, and per-criterion blocking via config') does not mention INDETERMINATE configurability — an omission that could cause the implementer to miss this config knob.

3. **Attestation CI signal vs. Follow-on F11**: AC bullet 6 ('checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal') is listed as an in-scope Success Criterion, yet F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers') is explicitly a follow-on. The plan cannot both deliver and defer the CI-check capability.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Rewrite the AC bug-exemption bullet to 'the gate routes around bug-type tickets (returns fast-pass without review); full bug-review logic is follow-on F6' to be unambiguous about what is and isn't implemented.
2. Add 'including INDETERMINATE configurability' to Success Criterion 4.
3. Either move the CI-check AC into F11 scope, or define a minimal in-scope deliverable (e.g. 'the signed attestation field is documented and machine-readable; CI integration helpers are F11') and update SC6 accordingly.

findings.location: NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT + SUCCESS CRITERIA section
SUCCESS CRITERIA SC5 vs. title 'ruleset-enforced fail-closed review gate'
findings.finding: SC5 expands the scope beyond the title's singular focus on the 'ruleset-enforced fail-closed review gate' by also requiring changes to the review-gate allowlist (moving docs/**, skills, behavior-driving config from exempt to reviewable). This is meaningful new policy enforcement logic — not just hardening the gate mechanism — and it is not reflected in the title. It could equally be read as a prerequisite to correctness (a gate that exempts too much isn't truly fail-closed), so a contradiction is not certain, but the mismatch is real enough to flag. Needs the author to confirm whether SC5 is in-scope or should be a parallel/follow-on story.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) retitle to something like 'Implement ruleset-enforced fail-closed review gate with corrected allowlist policy' to make SC5 explicit in scope, or (b) split SC5 into a separate child story with its own ticket if allowlist policy change is considered independently deliverable.

findings.location: SEQUENCING section + SUCCESS CRITERIA (SC1–SC6) + stories 1-8 references
findings.finding: This is an epic that references 8 child stories (stories 1–8) and 6 success criteria (SC1–SC6) tied to a large set of experiments (V0.1–V0.12, E2, E5, E6', E8, E11, E13, E14, E15, R8), but the child stories themselves are not defined anywhere in the plan text — only their dependencies and sequencing are sketched. An agent executing this ticket has no concrete leaf-level tasks to pick up. The plan acknowledges it 'translates plan Phase 2 (§5)' from an external doc (docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md) that is not reproduced here. Without the child stories defined or linked inline, decomposition exists only in intent but not in executable form, which will cause rework when agents try to scope individual sessions.
findings.suggested_edit: For each of the 8 child stories, add at minimum: (1) a one-sentence goal, (2) the single deliverable artifact/change, (3) the named verify experiment(s), and (4) the course-correction trigger. If this is truly an epic tracker, mark it explicitly as 'epic/parent — do not assign to a single session' and ensure each child has its own ticket with those fields populated.
