But SC2 is phrased as an absolute ('fail-closes … in ALL THREE carriers') with no qualifier about mode, which could be read as requiring fail-closed behaviour before the enforce-flip story 7 arrives. These readings are mutually inconsistent: either the carriers are fail-closed before story 7 (contradicting warn-first) or SC2 is only evaluated at story-7 time (contradicting its placement as a pre-condition success criterion). (2) SEQUENCING says 'stories 1-4 may proceed in parallel', but SC2 requires all three carrier workflows to be fixed simultaneously under enforce mode. If the three carrier fixes are split across different parallel stories, the intermediate state (some carriers fixed, some not) may violate SC2 transiently. The plan does not acknowledge or manage this window.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Add a mode qualifier to SC2: 'SC2 is evaluated at enforce-flip time (story 7); in warn mode stories 1–6 may exit non-zero only as advisory.' (2) Either assign all three carrier fail-closed fixes to the same story or add a note acknowledging the transient partial-fix window is acceptable because warn mode is advisory.

findings.location: SUCCESS CRITERIA section; SC1 ('Verified live: an allowlisted-only PR2 carrying un-reviewed content is BLOCKED'); SC2 ('Verified: a scope-stripped/expired token makes the gate RED'); SC3 ('the merge-pipeline-checks->review-gate swap is reflected on BOTH required-checks.txt and the live ruleset'); each story's 'Live-verify each story on real GitHub'
findings.finding: Multiple acceptance criteria explicitly require live GitHub state validation (live ruleset reads, live PR2 traffic, real PAT token expiry tests, live required-checks.txt parity) that cannot be completed within a single working session by an agent. SC1 requires a real PR2 to be BLOCKED by a live ruleset enforcement; SC2 requires a scoped/expired token to be observed making a gate RED in three live CI carriers; SC3 requires comparing live GitHub ruleset state to a local file. These are host-live operations that depend on external state the agent cannot control or simulate. Additionally, SC4 references 'coverage ledger warm-hits across re-runs' — verifying cache warm-hits requires multiple sequential CI runs. None of these can be simulated with provided auth in-session, and the plan's DELIVERY PRINCIPLES explicitly say 'validate on live PR2 traffic BEFORE any enforce-flip,' confirming the intent is real live-environment validation, not simulation.
findings.suggested_edit: For each SC that depends on live GitHub state, add a complementary in-session verifiable check: e.g., SC1 → 'the gate script exits nonzero when given a synthetic SHA list with no coverage entry (unit-testable)'; SC2 → 'the wrapper script exits nonzero when TOKEN env var is empty (unit-testable)'; SC3 → 'required-checks.txt diff against a fixture matches expected set.' Separate the 'live verify' steps into a named post-delivery smoke-test phase that a human operator runs, and make those steps advisory rather than part of the agent-completable acceptance criteria.

findings.location: SUCCESS CRITERIA (SC1–SC6) vs. SEQUENCING section; 'story 8 (UX) is independent'; SC6 references 'UX correctness fixes land (descriptive PR1 titles; sprint draft-umbrella lifecycle)'
findings.finding: SC6 references UX work (descriptive PR1 titles, sprint draft-umbrella lifecycle) which maps to story 8, but the plan body does not describe the UX changes in detail — only the sequencing note mentions story 8 exists. Without seeing the individual story descriptions, it is impossible to confirm whether SC6's UX sub-criteria ('descriptive PR1 titles,' 'sprint draft-umbrella lifecycle') map to described work or are orphan criteria. Similarly, SC5's allowlist changes ('behavior-bearing surfaces removed from allowlist') are referenced in the success criteria but not described in the plan body's mechanism. The terminology is also inconsistently applied: 'merge-pipeline-checks' (SC3) vs. 'review-gate' (SC1/SC3) vs. 'llm-review' (SC3) — the relationship between these three check names is only implied, never explicitly defined in the plan body. 'R8' (SC2, SC3, delivery principles) is used without being defined in this plan (presumably a story ID, but unexplained here).
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Terminology/Glossary section that maps: merge-pipeline-checks → review-gate → llm-review and clarifies the rename/swap relationship. Define R8 inline (e.g., 'R8 = the enforce-flip story, story 7'). For SC5 and SC6, add a one-paragraph description in the plan body explaining the allowlist mechanism and the UX changes so no criteria are orphaned.

findings.location: NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT section; SUCCESS CRITERIA preamble
Title vs. plan body; NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT vs. SUCCESS CRITERIA; SEQUENCING vs. DELIVERY PRINCIPLES
findings.finding: The plan body faithfully serves the stated title ('ruleset-enforced fail-closed review gate on the two-tier flow'). The NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT, SUCCESS CRITERIA, and SEQUENCING sections all consistently describe hardening the two-tier flow. No extra unrelated scope is introduced, and no element of the title promise is left uncovered. The warn-first delivery principle is consistent with the stated single enforce-flip as the hard-to-reverse step.

findings.location: SUCCESS CRITERIA (SC1–SC5); DELIVERY PRINCIPLES ('Live-verify each story on real GitHub'); each SC's experiment reference (E2, E13, V0.3a, V0.11, E5, E8, E11, etc.)
findings.finding: The testing plan relies almost entirely on live GitHub experiment references (E-series/V-series) that are external documents — the plan does not describe the test inputs, assertions, or pass/fail conditions for any of them inline. There is no RED-before-GREEN ordering described (no 'write a failing test first' or 'confirm gate is RED before flipping enforce'). Failure-path and boundary cases are named at a high level (SC2: 'scope-stripped/expired token'; SC4: 'coverage ledger warm-hits') but the plan does not describe HOW these will be exercised: what script, what fixture data, what expected exit code, what assertion captures the result. SC4 mentions 'no flaky-check team block' but gives no reproducibility mechanism. Without the E/V experiment docs being reproduced here, an executor cannot know what constitutes a passing test.
findings.suggested_edit: For each SC, inline the critical test cases as a brief table: Input | Expected behavior | How verified. E.g., SC2: 'Given TOKEN='' in enforce mode → script exits 1 with message containing PRECONDITION_FAIL; verified by running wrapper with env TOKEN= in CI dry-run.' This makes the test plan executor-independent of the external experiment docs. At minimum, add a sentence per SC naming the concrete assertion (exit code, log pattern, GitHub API response field) that constitutes PASS.

findings.location: NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT ('the enforcing checks must fail-closed'); SC2 ('ALL THREE carriers — review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml'); SC3 ('live main ruleset requires review-gate and retains llm-review'); SC4 ('coverage ledger warm-hits across re-runs'); DELIVERY PRINCIPLES ('R8 RED is repo-wide during the live<->file skew window')
findings.finding: The plan makes several implicit assumptions about pre-existing codebase state that cannot be confirmed without access to the live repository: (1) It assumes review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, and ruleset-invariants.yml already exist and are wired as CI carriers — if any are missing or named differently, SC2 cannot be tested as written. (2) It assumes a 'coverage ledger' mechanism already exists with warm-hit caching behavior (SC4). (3) It assumes the live main ruleset already contains 'llm-review' as a required check that must be 'retained' (SC3). (4) It assumes 'R8' (the live<->file skew window) is a known, pre-existing operational concept that the executor will recognize. (5) The ADR reference (0020-two-tier-hardening-over-merge-queue.md) and option-a-pivot-plan.md are cited as source of truth but not summarized — the plan assumes the executor has read and internalized these docs.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Prerequisites / Verified Starting State' section that explicitly asserts the confirmed state: list the three YML files with their current paths; state the current live ruleset required-checks list; confirm the coverage ledger file path and caching mechanism; and summarize the key decisions from ADR-0020 and option-a-pivot-plan.md that this plan builds on. This converts unverified assumptions into checked preconditions.

findings.location: Plan description: 'Source of truth: docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md … and docs/adr/0020-two-tier-hardening-over-merge-queue.md'; also 'See each story's dependency links'
findings.finding: The plan is written as an epic-level summary and repeatedly defers critical design decisions to external documents ('source of truth: docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md', 'See each story's dependency links') that are not reproduced here. An executing agent would need to halt and fetch those documents to know: (1) what the individual story acceptance criteria are, (2) what files to edit per story, (3) what the named experiments (E2, E5, E6', E8, E11, E13, E14, E15, V0.3a-post-fix, R8, etc.) require in terms of concrete steps. The plan names experiments by label (e.g., 'V0.12', 'E-compute-hash') without defining them anywhere in the ticket itself. The warn-mode / enforce-flip boundary is described at a high level but the specific CI YAML changes, ruleset API calls, and ledger schema are all deferred to the external doc. This constitutes multiple TBD/research-deferred decision points that would force an agent to stop.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the per-story acceptance criteria and concrete step lists (file names, API calls, YAML stanzas) for each story into the ticket, or confirm this ticket is intentionally an epic-index and each child story carries the executable detail — in which case flag it clearly as 'do not execute directly; execute child tickets only'.

findings.location: SUCCESS CRITERIA section (SC1–SC6) and DELIVERY PRINCIPLES (course-correction triggers, completion-verifier)
SEQUENCING section: 'stories 1-4 may proceed in parallel after start; story 5 depends on 1+2+4; story 6 depends on 5; story 7 (enforce-flip) depends on 1-6 ALL verified; story 8 (UX) is independent'
findings.finding: The ticket names at least 8 child stories with a non-trivial dependency graph, plus references to 12+ named experiments and a live ruleset swap (story 7) that is explicitly called 'the only hard-to-reverse step'. As a single ticket/epic this is far too large to execute coherently in one session. However, decomposition into children IS acknowledged ('stories 1-4', 'story 5', etc.). The critical gap is that none of the child stories are actually linked or described in the plan body — their acceptance criteria, file targets, and verification steps are all in an external document. This means the epic-level ticket cannot serve as an actionable work item and the decomposition is nominal rather than real within the ticket system.
findings.suggested_edit: Create 8 explicit child tickets (one per story), each with its own acceptance criteria, named file edits, and experiment-verification step. The epic should only contain the sequencing graph and SC1–SC6 rollup. Mark this ticket as 'Epic — not directly executable'.

findings.location: SC2: 'all review-enforcing CI wrappers fail-closed … in ALL THREE carriers — review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml'; SC3: 'live-parity guard'; SC1: 'per-SHA coverage' ledger
findings.finding: The plan introduces at least three separate CI YAML files all participating in the review gate, plus a coverage ledger, plus a live-parity guard, plus a ruleset-invariants check. Without access to the codebase it is impossible to confirm whether simpler consolidation is possible (e.g., a single workflow with jobs, rather than three separate YAMLs each needing their own fail-closed wrapper). The per-SHA ledger ('coverage ledger warm-hits across re-runs') is a custom caching mechanism whose necessity vs. using GitHub's native job outputs/caches cannot be assessed from the plan alone. These may all be justified by the existing architecture, but the proliferation of enforcement layers is a flag worth confirming.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief rationale note in the plan (or ADR-0020) explaining why three separate YAML files are required rather than one workflow with three jobs sharing a single fail-closed harness.

findings.location: Entire plan; specifically SC3 ('required-checks.txt and the live ruleset'), SC5 ('docs/**, skills, behavior-driving config … removed from the review-gate allowlist'), DELIVERY PRINCIPLES ('admin PAT: provision/read')
findings.finding: The plan mentions editing 'required-checks.txt', the 'review-gate allowlist', 'review-coverage-invariant.yml', 'dangling-references.yml', 'ruleset-invariants.yml', and the 'live ruleset' via API — but no specific file paths, symbol names, or repository locations are given in the ticket body. It is impossible to verify from the plan text alone whether these files exist, what their current schema is, or whether there are additional callers (e.g., other workflows that import or depend on these YAMLs) that would also need updating. The blast radius of the live-ruleset swap (R8) is acknowledged as repo-wide but no inventory of affected workflows is provided.
findings.suggested_edit: List the full relative paths of every file to be created or modified (e.g., `.github/workflows/review-coverage-invariant.yml`, `docs/config/allowlist.txt`, etc.) and enumerate any workflows that currently reference the checks being renamed/replaced.

findings.location: Multiple cross-section contradictions detailed below
findings.finding:
Two internal tensions were found:

1. SEQUENCING vs. DELIVERY PRINCIPLES: The sequencing section says 'stories 1-4 may proceed in parallel after start' but the DELIVERY PRINCIPLES say 'every story ships its enforcing logic in warn/advisory mode and is validated on live PR2 traffic BEFORE any enforce-flip'. Parallel execution of stories 1-4 means their warn-mode validations would also need to run concurrently on the same live PR2 traffic stream. The plan does not explain how concurrent warn-mode experiments on the same PR2 stream are isolated from one another, creating a potential interference risk that the course-correction trigger mechanism does not address.

2. SC4 vs. SC5 (allowlist scope): SC4 states 'a commit whose diff is entirely within the event-sourced ticket store is exempt' (implying ticket-store paths are allowlisted); SC5 states 'behavior-bearing surfaces (docs/**…) are removed from the review-gate allowlist and become reviewable'. If any docs live inside or alongside the ticket store directory, the boundary between SC4's exemption and SC5's coverage expansion is ambiguous and could produce contradictory gate behavior depending on path overlap.
findings.suggested_edit: For tension 1: add a note to the SEQUENCING section specifying that parallel story execution requires separate PR2 fixtures or branch namespacing to avoid experiment interference. For tension 2: explicitly define the directory boundary between 'event-sourced ticket store' (exempt) and 'docs/**' (reviewable), e.g., by listing the exact glob patterns for each.

findings.location: Plan description: 'Source of truth: docs/handoff/option-a-pivot-plan.md ... and docs/adr/0020-two-tier-hardening-over-merge-queue.md'; stories 1–8 referenced but not enumerated
findings.finding: The epic plan continuously defers substantive design decisions to external documents that are not reproduced or summarized in this ticket. An executing agent is told to implement 'Phase 2 (§5) + review-#4/#6 prerequisites' and named stories 1–8 but none of those stories, their acceptance criteria, or their implementation steps are spelled out here. Critical design questions left unanswered in-ticket include: (1) What exact GitHub Ruleset configuration constitutes the required-check swap? (2) What is the schema/interface for the 'coverage ledger'? (3) What constitutes 'proven reviewed per-SHA'? (4) Which files exactly are on the allowlist vs. reviewable surface (SC5)? (5) What is the 'completion-verifier' and how is it invoked? An agent would need to stop and read two external docs before any action is possible.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the per-story task lists (inputs, steps, outputs, acceptance criteria) directly into this ticket, or create one child ticket per story with full detail, and reduce this epic to a pure tracking/sequencing record that is not itself actionable.

findings.location: SUCCESS CRITERIA section (SC1–SC6) and SEQUENCING paragraph
findings.finding: The success criteria name specific experiments (E2, E5, E6', E8, E11, E13, E14, E15, V0.3a-post-fix, V0.11, R8) as the verification mechanism but provide no description of what those experiments do, how to run them, what a PASS output looks like, or where their results are recorded. 'Completion-verifier PASS' is mentioned in the SC preamble but the verifier itself is undefined in this ticket. A reviewer (or CI bot) cannot determine doneness without access to the pivot-plan document. Additionally, SC3 says 'the R8 live-parity guard fail-closes under enforce' but the steps to create or validate R8 are absent. The SEQUENCING paragraph lists dependency ordering (story 5 depends on 1+2+4, etc.) but there are no named deliverables per story to confirm those dependencies are satisfied.
findings.suggested_edit: For each SC, add: (a) the concrete observable output (log line, GitHub UI state, file content), (b) who/what runs the verification, and (c) a link or inline summary of each named experiment. The 'completion-verifier' should be named as a specific script or CI job with its expected exit-code/output.

findings.location: SEQUENCING paragraph: 'stories 1–8'; plan description: 'This epic translates plan Phase 2 (§5) + review-#4/#6 prerequisites into shippable phases'
findings.finding: This ticket is a multi-story epic (at minimum 8 stories with parallel and sequential dependencies) presented as a single ticket without child-ticket decomposition. The stories are referenced by number but their scope, implementation steps, and acceptance criteria are entirely absent from the ticket. This makes the ticket non-executable as a unit of work. The plan acknowledges the complexity ('single enforce-flip … is the only hard-to-reverse step', 'warn-first', per-story 'COURSE-CORRECTION TRIGGER') but does not decompose into children. Even as a pure tracking epic, the absence of child tickets means the sequencing dependency graph cannot be validated or acted upon.
findings.suggested_edit: Create one child ticket per story (at minimum: story 7 'enforce-flip' and stories that carry the allowlist/ledger/ruleset changes as separate vertical slices). This epic ticket should then carry only: goal, non-negotiable requirement, sequencing map, SC checklist, and links to children.

findings.location: SC1: 'a single always-runs required check `review-gate`'; SC2: 'ALL THREE carriers — review-coverage-invariant.yml, dangling-references.yml, ruleset-invariants.yml'; SC3: 'live ruleset requires `review-gate` and retains `llm-review`'
findings.finding: The plan introduces at least three YAML workflow files plus a new 'review-gate' required check plus a 'coverage ledger' plus a 'completion-verifier', alongside the existing llm-review check. Without access to the codebase it is impossible to confirm whether GitHub's native Branch Protection / Ruleset required-status-checks feature (which already fail-closes on missing checks) is being fully leveraged or whether bespoke wrapper logic is being written to replicate behavior GitHub provides natively. If the three carrier YAMLs are each re-implementing fail-closed sentinel logic rather than simply being listed as required checks, that would be an NIH anti-pattern. Similarly, a 'coverage ledger' is a new stateful artifact whose necessity vs. a simpler PR-label or check-run approach is not justified in the plan.
findings.suggested_edit: In the plan, explicitly justify why each custom YAML carrier is needed beyond native Ruleset required-status-checks. If the ledger is a cache of check-run results, confirm no GitHub API primitive (e.g., check-run conclusions stored on SHAs) already covers the use case.

findings.location: SC5: 'behavior-bearing surfaces (docs/**, skills, behavior-driving config) are removed from the review-gate allowlist'; SC3: 'the merge-pipeline-checks->review-gate swap is reflected on BOTH required-checks.txt and the live ruleset'
findings.finding: The plan names 'required-checks.txt', 'review-coverage-invariant.yml', 'dangling-references.yml', 'ruleset-invariants.yml', and the 'review-gate' check, but does not enumerate: (a) which existing callers reference 'merge-pipeline-checks' that will break when it is removed, (b) whether 'required-checks.txt' is a custom file or a GitHub-managed artifact, (c) what the current allowlist contents are, and (d) which other workflows import or depend on the three carrier YAMLs. The blast radius of the allowlist change (SC5) is unacknowledged — removing docs/** from the allowlist will affect every open docs-only PR2 in flight at the moment of the flip. Without codebase access, the named files cannot be confirmed to exist or be complete.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'files touched' section listing every file being created/modified/deleted with its current role. For the allowlist change, explicitly call out the in-flight PR impact and add it to the course-correction trigger or the R8 runbook.

findings.location: Multiple sections: DELIVERY PRINCIPLES 'warn-first'; SEQUENCING 'story 7 (enforce-flip) depends on 1-6 ALL verified'; SC3 'the merge-pipeline-checks->review-gate swap'; NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT 'every commit reaching main must pass … a hard GitHub gate'
findings.finding: Three internal tensions are present: (1) The NON-NEGOTIABLE REQUIREMENT declares every commit to main must currently pass a hard gate, but the DELIVERY PRINCIPLES mandate a warn-first phase that ships advisory-mode checks first. These are not contradictory per se, but the plan never states the current state of main — if main already has unreviewed commits, the warn→enforce pipeline would have to back-fill, which is unaddressed. (2) SC3 says 'live ruleset requires review-gate and retains llm-review', but the plan also says 'merge-pipeline-checks->review-gate swap', implying merge-pipeline-checks is removed. The fate of llm-review as a required check post-swap is contradicted between SC3 (retained) and the swap language (replaced). (3) SEQUENCING says 'stories 1–4 may proceed in parallel after start; story 5 depends on 1+2+4' — story 3 is excluded from story 5's dependency list. If story 3 produces an artifact consumed by story 5 (plausible given unnamed story contents), this is a gap; if story 3 is truly independent of story 5, the plan should explicitly state that.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Add a 'current state of main' section clarifying whether the gate is being introduced or upgraded. (2) Clarify in SC3 whether llm-review remains as a separate required check alongside review-gate or is subsumed. (3) Explicitly state story 3's relationship to story 5.
